title: More than 8 million people rely on income support, driven by a growing number of mental health claims
full_text: Other Editions 06 May 2026 More than 8 million people rely on income support, driven by a growing number of mental health claims Australian Broadcasting Corporation News national work reporter Bronwyn Herbert 5 May 2026 1002 words English ABCNEW (c) 2026 Australian Broadcasting Corporation More than 8 million Australians now rely on some form of income support, putting pressure on a safety net that was never designed to take on so many, but insurers say earlier intervention and unified assessment processes would improve the system. With around 2 million more people now accessing payments than a decade ago, the surge is being driven by Australians whose physical and mental health has deteriorated to the point they can no longer work. Mental ill health now accounts for about one-third of claims across Australia's major income support schemes, according to data compiled by researchers at Monash University and SuperFriend for the Council of Australian Life Insurers (CALI). "We are now faced with a major national productivity challenge," said Christine Cupitt, chief executive of CALI. "It's not just one part of the system under pressure — the entire safety net is under strain." The report found demand had increased across all 11 of Australia's income support systems, spanning employer-provided sick leave and workers' compensation to social security payments, superannuation and life insurance claims. Employers remain the biggest contributor, with paid sick leave supporting around 7.5 million Australians, typically for short periods of about three weeks. However, more people are exhausting sick leave and moving into longer term support, where the chances of returning to work diminish. "Mental health has really changed the profile of these claims," Ms Cupitt said. "It's now one of the leading drivers of people needing income support, and these claims tend to be more complex and longer in duration." 'It took me completely by surprise' For 30‑year-old Lauren Frahamer, the statistics are reflected in her daily life, after long COVID meant she could no longer work. Before she fell ill, Ms Frahamer was a stage manager on major musical theatre productions, working around 50 hours a week, often nights and weekends, while maintaining an intense fitness routine of gym sessions, swimming, running and yoga. "I was very active," she said. "Work was a big part of my life." She contracted COVID-19 in December 2021, during an outbreak that spread through her workplace. The acute illness was brutal but unremarkable — weeks of fever, pain and exhaustion — but recovery never came. "I tried to go back to work for about a month and just couldn't do it," she said. "I had extreme fatigue. I had brain fog. I couldn't remember a sentence well enough to pass a message on. My job depends on focus for hours at a time, and I just didn't have it." As the symptoms dragged on, Ms Frahamer said confusion and fear set in. "I was mentally unwell through that whole process," she said. "At first, I was almost numb. Then as my brain started coming back online, I realised something was seriously wrong. "I hadn't had a major health event before — it took me completely by surprise." Eventually, she resigned, moved back to Melbourne to be closer to family, and began treatment through an allied health clinic specialising in complex, long-term conditions. The turning point came when her partner at the time was made redundant. "That's when I really had to look at what support existed," she said. "I didn't even know if long COVID would be recognised. I had no idea if it was claimable." In mid-2022, Ms Frahamer was granted partial income protection — support she said kept her from losing independence altogether. "It didn't take long to burn through my savings," she said. "Rent, life costs, appointments, medications — it adds up fast. Without income protection, I would've had to move back in with my parents and be completely dependent again." Ms Frahamer now works nine hours a week in client support at the same allied health clinic that once treated her — about 40 per cent of her former capacity. "It's been about a year at this level," she said. But she's determined to take steps to work full-time again. "Work gives you purpose," she said. "When it's taken away through no fault of your own … it's hard." Earlier intervention needed Associate Professor Ross Iles, a Monash University researcher and chief research adviser at SuperFriend, said the number of working-age Australians needing income support had risen steadily. "Mental health seems to be having a bigger toll on people's ability to work," he said. One of the biggest problems with the way income support is set up is fragmentation, according to Mr Iles. Each scheme has different eligibility rules, medical evidence requirements and application processes. "If someone's workers' compensation claim is rejected and they need to turn to insurance or social security, that's a whole new application," he said. "New forms, new medical assessments, new processes. When you're unwell, that burden is enormous." He said the longer a person was out of work, the less likely they were to return. Delays in treatment, claim processing and coordinated support reduced recovery prospects and increased long-term costs. "We're spending close to $80 billion a year on income support," Mr Iles said. "And that figure doesn't even count lost productivity. The best outcome is helping people stay connected to work or return when they can." Life insurers say the current system intervenes too late. They're calling for earlier, better-coordinated support while people are still working as well as a government-led overhaul that links Australia's 11 income support systems to employers, insurers and public agencies. It would mean nationally consistent mental health definitions, shared data, better identification of people at risk of long-term work loss, and smoother transitions between supports. "There's a burning platform here," Ms Cuppitt said. "We can't afford systems operating in silos while more Australians get sick and leave the workforce." summary: Over 8 million Australians rely on income support, with mental health claims now constituting about one-third of these. The increase in claims is attributed to deteriorating physical and mental health, leading to longer-term support needs. Insurers advocate for earlier intervention and a unified assessment process to improve the current fragmented income support system.
publication:

heading: Life Insurance
key_points: Mental health claims account for one-third of support
Insurers call for coordinated support and system overhaul
relevance: This is relevant as it highlights the growing impact of mental health on income support systems, which affects risk assessment and financial planning in the insurance sector.
